name: MacTutor
biography:
Louis Benjamin Francoeur was born into a famous Paris family of musicians. His father, Louis-Joseph Francoeur (1738 - 1804) , was the son of Louis Francoeur (1692 - 1745) , who was the leader of the Vingt-Quatre Violons du Roi, and the nephew of Fran횄짠ois Francoeur who also played in the Vingt-Quatre Violons. Louis-Joseph, Louis and Fran횄짠ois all also played in the Op횄짤ra orchestra. At the time Louis Benjamin was born, his father was ma횄짰tre of the Op횄짤ra orchestra and he became its director in 1779 . Louis Benjamin was brought up in Saint Cloud, a wealthy commune to the west of Paris, and educated at the College d'Harcourt. This ancient educational establishment was founded in 1280 but was renamed the Lyc횄짤e Saint-Louis in 1820 ; it is today known for its exceptional teaching. He became a clerk to a notary in 1790 but, by this time, Paris was in turmoil as the Revolution took its course. The Opera had been the Royal Opera until 1792 when it became a public utility independent of the king. Louis Francoeur resigned as director of the Opera and tried to set up a new company with the King's support. He founded the Francoeur-Cellerier company in 1792 and Louis Benjamin he took a position as deputy cashier in his father's opera company. Like other members of his family he was exceptionally talented musically, so working for an opera company was a pleasant task.
The execution of Louis XVI on 21 January 1793 meant that the Francoeur-Cellerier company was short-lived and a financial disaster. Louis Benjamin lost his position and, in September 1793 , his father was imprisoned because of his royal connections; Louis-Joseph Francoeur spent nearly a year in prison and when he was released he had no pension and was heavily in debt. Now, as well as internal strife, France was faced with attacks from the Austrians and Prussians and, from 1793 , Louis Benjamin was required to serve in the French Revolutionary army. At first he was sent as a sergeant major to a regiment of the French Guard stationed at P횄짤ronne in northern France. Following this he served in the Auvergne Regiment which was at Maubeuge trying to defend against the Austrian army under the command of Prince Josias of Coburg. Francoeur, however, became involved in a musical role at Maubeuge. He had risen to 'Chef de brigade', a rank equivalent to colonel, but on hearing that technicians for the arms factories were being recruited in Paris, he quickly returned there. Having failed to get such a position, he sadly set off for Maubeuge in August 1794 but when he heard that students were being sought for the new 횄혟cole Centrale des Travaux Publics ( soon to be renamed the 횄혟cole Polytechnique ) he returned again to Paris.
Passing the entrance examination, he studied mathematics with Gaspard Monge at the 횄혟cole Centrale des Travaux Publics, beginning in November 1794 . He was a brilliant pupil, completing his studies in 1797 . Then he was appointed to the School of Geographical Engineers, which allowed him to remain in Paris to give private lessons to favoured students, such as J횄짤r횄쨈me Bonaparte, the youngest brother of Napoleon Bonaparte. He also studied botany under the direction of Ren횄짤 Louiche Desfontaines and Jean-Louis Thuillier while he waited to continue an army career as a geographer. However, Gaspard de Prony had realised that Francoeur was a talented mathematician and lecturer so, in 1798 , he appointed him as r횄짤p횄짤titeur d'analyse to teach an analysis course for Sylvestre Lacroix . De Prony was the first teacher of mechanics at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique and Francoeur, as his teaching assistant, quickly produced the substantial Trait횄짤 de m횄짤chanique 횄짤l횄짤mentaire 창혫혟 ( Treatise on elementary mechanics ) (1801) to serve as an introduction to the subject for students at the 횄혟cole. His academic position saved Francoeur from the dangers and discomforts of army life but he carried on with both a military and an academic career. He was theoretically made a sub-lieutenant in the artillery in 1802 although by this time he was taking no active part in the military. In 1804 he was appointed professor of mathematics at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, then in 1805 he became professor of mathematics at the Lyc횄짤e Charlemagne. In 1808 he was made professor of mathematics at the Facult횄짤 des Sciences in Paris, a post he was to hold in addition to others until 1845 .
Francoeur had many duties in his capacity as an examiner for the 횄혟cole Polytechnique and he often travelled in France and Italy carrying out these duties. The French, under Napoleon, fought inconclusive battles and campaigns in various parts of Spain and Portugal during 1809 - 10 . Francoeur was sent on this Peninsular Campaign but he broke a leg in an accident with a carriage. During 1810 he spent four months in bed as a result of this accident. In 1811 a comet appeared causing great interest with the public. It was first observed in March of that year, but it was not until September that it was a bright object in the evening sky, reaching its maximum brightness in October. Francoeur became interested in astronomy as a result of this comet and was appointed professor of astronomy at the Ath횄짤n횄짤e in Paris. However, in 1814 Napoleon Bonaparte was defeated and the Bourbon Restoration government was set up. In 1815 over 50 , 000 civil servants were sacked, and 15 , 000 army officers dismissed. Francoeur lost his chair at the Lyc횄짤e Charlemagne and also his position at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique. In 1816 he became a founding member of the Soci횄짤t횄짤 pour l'enseignement 횄짤l횄짤mentaire, a society whose aim was to promote elementary instruction in schools. He later became secretary of the Society. Francoeur encouraged learning in schools and he did his part writing books, particularly Le dessin lin횄짤aire d'apr횄짢s la m횄짤thode de l'enseignement mutuel (1819) which popularised line drawing in French schools.
Francoeur is famed as a writer of excellent texts mostly based on lecture courses he gave. He published his mechanics book Trait횄짤 de m횄짤canique 횄짤l횄짤mentaire, 횄혻 l'usage des 횄짤l횄짢ves de l'횄혟cole Polytechnique, r횄짤dig횄짤 d'apr횄짢s les m횄짤thodes de R Prony 창혫혟 ( Treatise on elementary mechanics, for use by students of the Ecole Polytechnique, prepared by the methods of R Prony ) in 1800 , an elementary course of mathematics in two volumes Cours complet de math횄짤matiques pures 창혫혟 ( A complete course of pure mathematics ) in 1809 , and two astronomy texts l'Uranographie, ou Trait횄짤 횄짤l횄짤mentaire d'astronomie, 횄혻 l'usage des personnes peu vers횄짤es dans les math횄짤matiques, accompagn횄짤 de planisph횄짢res 창혫혟 ( Star mapping or treatise on elementary astronomy, the use of beginners in mathematics, with maps of the stars ) (1812) and Astronomie pratique, usage et composition de la Connaissance des temps 창혫혟 ( Practical Astronomy, use and composition of the Time Knowledge ) (1830) . His other books include 횄혟l횄짤mens de statique 창혫혟 ( Elements of statics ) (1810) , La goniom횄짤trie 창혫혟 ( Direction finding ) (1820) , L'enseignment du dessin lin횄짤aire 창혫혟 ( Teaching of linear drawing ) (1827) , Astronomie practique 창혫혟 ( Practical Astronomy ) (1830) , 횄혟l횄짤mens de technologie 창혫혟 ( Elements of technology ) (1833) , G횄짤od횄짤sie, ou Trait횄짤 de la figure de la terre et de ses parties, comprenant la topographie, l'arpentage, le nivellement, la g횄짤omorphie terrestre et astronomique, la construction des cartes, la navigation, le횄짠ons donn횄짤es 횄혻 la Facult횄짤 des sciences de Paris 창혫혟 ( Geodesy, or treatise on the shape of the earth and its parts, including topography, surveying, leveling, terrestrial and astronomical geomorphism, drawing maps, navigation, lessons taught at the Faculty of Science Paris ) (1835) , Alg횄짢bre sup횄짤rieure 창혫혟 ( Higher Algebra ) (1838) , M횄짤moire sur l'Ar횄짤om횄짤trie 창혫혟 ( Memoir on the measurement of area ) (1842) , and Trait횄짤 d'arithm횄짤tique appliqu횄짤e 횄혻 la banque, au commerce, 횄혻 l'industrie, etc., recueil de m횄짤thodes propres 횄혻 r횄짤soudre les probl횄짢mes et 횄혻 abr횄짤ger les calculs num횄짤riques 창혫혟 ( Treatise on arithmetic  applied to banking, commerce, industry, etc., collection of methods to solve problems and to shorten numerical calculations ) (1845) . Finally, let us also mention his text Flore parisienne ou Description des caract횄짢res de toutes les plantes qui croissent naturellement aux environs de Paris, distribu횄짤es suivant la m횄짤thode du jardin des plantes 창혫혟 ( Description of Parisiam Flora or characters of all plants that grow naturally around Paris, distributed according to the method of garden plants ) (1800 - 01) .
Many of these works were dedicated to famous mathematicians, for example the 횄혟l횄짤mens de statique 창혫혟 ( Elements of statics ) was dedicated to Pierre-Simon Laplace , the G횄짤od횄짤sie 창혫혟 ( Geodesy ) was dedicated to Louis Puissant , and the fifth edition l'Uranographie 창혫혟 ( Star mapping ) published in 1837 was dedicated to Fran횄짠ois Arago , the fourth edition of 1828 having been dedicated to Sadi Carnot who died in 1832 .
It was for his work in line drawing that Francoeur became most famous, the method he proposed being known as "Francoeur's method". It was first used in a school in Libourne in Aquitaine in south-western France which had been founded by 횄혟lie, duc Decazes. His method was published in 1819 in Le dessin lin횄짤aire d'apr횄짢s la m횄짤thode de l'enseignement mutuel but, before publication of this book, Francoeur had submitted a report on his method to the Soci횄짤t횄짤 pour l'enseignement 횄짤l횄짤mentaire on 22 July 1818 . Extracts from this report are given in [ 5 ] . Francoeur's method was used in countries other than France. For example William B Fowle translated Francoeur's book into English and published it as An Introduction to Linear Drawing: Translated from the French of M. Francoeur, and Adapted to the Use of Public Schools in the United States (1825) [ 3 ] :- Fowle's text, which was used by students attending Cambridge High School as early as 1841 , teaches drawing according to the mastery of basic geometric shapes. Fowle was a staunch advocate of mandatory drawing classes in public schools and also served as editor of the 'Common School Journal'. An anonymous review of a later edition of Fowle's translation of Francoeur's text appeared in 1842 the Common School Journal :- The little work upon 'Linear Drawing' exhibits a plan of teaching large classes at once, by means of a black-board, all the elements of the art of drawing. The teacher stands at the board, draws and explains every line - linear, oblique, curved - and then proceeds to copy from patterns, or objects - explaining every thing in the process - while the pupils copy each line, [ and so on ] , upon their slates or paper, as she slowly proceeds. Francoeur, in his report of 1818 , describes how the students are divided into four classes [ 5 ] :- The lower [ class ] traces the lines, parallels, perpendiculars, triangles, etc. In the second class, students make circles, regular polygons and plane figures that depend on them. In the third, they mimic perspective in some three-dimensional bodies, such as pyramids, prisms, cylinders, cones, spheres, etc. In the fourth class, they draw some architectural features, vases and ornaments of their choice. We explained as the beginning of this biography that Francoeur came from an exceptionally musical family so it will come as no surprise that he also reported to the Soci횄짤t횄짤 pour l'enseignement 횄짤l횄짤mentaire on the teaching of singing and the Society adopted the method he proposed.
Among the honours Francoeur received was election to the Acad횄짤mie des Sciences in 1842 . He was elected to the many other Academies including St Petersburg, Lisbon, Edinburgh, Rouen, Lyon, Cambra, and Toulouse.
From 1840 an illness of the larynx curtailed his activities and he visited many spas looking for a cure by taking the waters. The illness forced him to retire from his chair in the Faculty of Sciences in 1845 . He health deteriorated further through a disease of his spinal chord and eventually he went blind forcing him to stop all his activities. He died at Fontainbleau and was buried at Chatillon-la-Borde ( Seine-et-Marne ) .
Finally we note that Francoeur's son, Isidore Francoeur, the author of [ 1 ] , was himself a professor of mathematics at the Coll횄짢ge Chaptal and at the 횄혟cole des Beaux-Arts. He bequeathed to them his library and that of his father. Isidore Francoeur's widow made a gift to set up the mathematics prize the Prix Francoeur.
